facts.text: Park Slope Historic District covers 2,575 buildings across about 40 blocks, making it New York's largest landmark neighborhood.
The Park Slope branch of the Brooklyn Public Library, a Carnegie‑built landmark from 1905‑06, still serves the community.
Flatbush Avenue’s 1875 streetcar line and Atlantic Avenue Company’s Fifth and Ninth Avenue lines once crisscrossed Park Slope.
Preservationists secured landmark status for many blocks of row houses, brownstones, and Queen Anne, Renaissance Revival, and Romanesque mansions.
After the 1973 landmark district designation, gentrification accelerated, drawing young professionals into Park Slope’s historic streets.
In 2007, the American Planning Association named Park Slope one of America’s greatest neighborhoods for its architecture, diversity, and engaged residents.
